texts.label: text
texts.orig: The topology and chemical functionality of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) make them promising candidates for membrane gas separation; however, few meet the criteria for industrial applications, that is, selectivity of > 30 for CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2. This paper reports on a dense CAU-10-H MOF membrane that is exceptionally CO2-selective (ideal selectivity of 42 for CO 2 / N2 and 95 for CO2/CH4). The proposed membrane also achieves the highest CO2 permeability (approximately 500 Barrer) among existing pure MOF membranes with CO2/CH4 selectivity exceeding 30. State-of-the-art atomistic simulations provide valuable insights into the outstanding separation performance of CAU-10-H at the molecular level. Adsorbent-adsorbate Coulombic interactions are identified as a crucial factor in the design of CO 2 -selective MOF membranes.

texts.orig: separation  of  CO 2 from  N 2 or  CH 4 ,  such as  liquid  absorption  and  cryogenic  distillation, are energy-intensive.  Membrane gas  separation  is  an  attractive  alternative, due to its simplicity and low energy overhead; however, industrial-scale CO2/N2 and  CO2/CH4  separations  are  only  viable using membranes with selectivity of > 30. [1] Polymers are the most intensively studied and most widely employed class of membrane  materials  with  demonstrable  CO 2 separation potential. One study presented totally amorphous poly(ethylene oxide) with  high  CO 2   selectivity  (48  for  CO 2 /N2 and  20  for  CO 2 /CH 4)  and  CO2  permeability  of  143  Barrer. [2] In  another  study, rigid  glassy  polyimide  achieved  a  selec- tivity of > 30 for both CO2/N2 and CO2/CH4; however, CO2 permeability was only 6.2 Barrer. [3]  A novel elastic block copolymer, poly(ether-block-amide)  containing  linear  chains  of  hard  polyamide  and  soft  polyether  segments  achieved  outstanding  selectivity of 50 for CO 2 /N2 and 11 for CO 2 /CH 4  with CO2 permeability of  110  Barrer. [4]   Unfortunately,  most  polymeric  membranes  are susceptible  to  CO 2 -induced  plasticization  under  aggressive  feed conditions (high-pressure or acidic environment), leading to a significant loss in selectivity. [5]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Glassy  as  well  as  crystalline  inorganic  ultramicroporous ( d < 0.7 nm) materials have recently been investigated as potential  membrane candidates. Glassy inorganic carbon molecular sieve  (CMS)  membranes  with  slit-shaped  pores  have  been shown to achieve CO2/N2 selectivity of > 30 and CO2/CH4 selectivity of > 80 with high CO2 permeability of > 10 3 Barrer. [6] Note, however, that most CMS membranes suffer a significant reduction in CO2 permeability over time, due to densification of the amorphous sp 2  hybridized carbon sheets. [7]  Organosilica membranes  are  another  class  of  glassy  inorganic  membranes  with high CO2/N2 selectivity ( > 100) and CO2/CH4 selectivity ( > 90). [8] Graphene oxide (GO) membranes have been reported to show high CO2/N2 selectivity (>50) under humid conditions; [9]  however, the high feed pressure can change the interlayer spacing between  the  GO  nanosheets  and  destabilize  the  GO  membranes. [10]   A  number  of  polycrystalline  zeolite  membranes have  demonstrated  good  CO2  separation  performance, [11]   but the range of applicable materials is dwarfed by that of another emerging  class  of  crystalline  porous  materials,  metal-organic frameworks (MOFs).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Despite  considerable  advances  in  the  development  of  MOF membranes for gas separation, [13]  few researchers have achieved the  CO2/N2  or  CO2/CH4  separation  performance  required for  industrial  applications,  that  is,  selectivity  beyond  30. [13c,14] This  can  largely  be  attributed  to  their  intrinsic  crystallinity, which  hinders  the  fabrication  of  low-defect  pure  MOF  membranes.  The  flexibility  of  the  MOF  framework [15] (referred  to as  breathing  or  gate-opening  effects)  also  makes  it  difficult  to predict molecular sieving capability based on the pore-limiting diameter (PLD). Moreover, the chemical composition of MOFs can  have  a  profound  influence  on  charge  distribution  within the  channels,  which  can  apply  an  attractive  or  repulsive  force on  molecules  with  a  quadrupole  moment,  such  as  CO 2 .  This Coulombic effect can either facilitate or inhibit molecular hopping  from  one  cage  to  another.  The  previous  computational studies have pointed out how the charge effect can slow down the diffusion of CO 2  in MOFs. [16]

[13c,14] This  can  largely  be  attributed  to  their  intrinsic  crystallinity, which  hinders  the  fabrication  of  low-defect  pure  MOF  membranes.  The  flexibility  of  the  MOF  framework [15] (referred  to as  breathing  or  gate-opening  effects)  also  makes  it  difficult  to predict molecular sieving capability based on the pore-limiting diameter (PLD). Moreover, the chemical composition of MOFs can  have  a  profound  influence  on  charge  distribution  within the  channels,  which  can  apply  an  attractive  or  repulsive  force on  molecules  with  a  quadrupole  moment,  such  as  CO 2 .  This Coulombic effect can either facilitate or inhibit molecular hopping  from  one  cage  to  another.  The  previous  computational studies have pointed out how the charge effect can slow down the diffusion of CO 2  in MOFs. [16]

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our  objective  in  the  current  study  was  to  create  a  highly CO2-selective MOF  membrane  with aluminum  hydroxide isophthalate MOF, CAU-10-H. [17]  We began by re-evaluating the PLD of CAU-10-H using experimental as well as computational methods.  We  then  fabricated  a  dense  low-defect  CAU-10-H membrane for single-gas and mixed-gas permeation by which to  assess  its  CO2/CH4 and CO2/N2 selectivity. We also sought to gain insight into the gas transport in the framework of CAU10-H by performing molecular simulations. The computational tools allowed for the molecular-level investigation of the charge effect on the CO 2  diffusion in CAU-10-H.

We also sought to gain insight into the gas transport in the framework of CAU10-H by performing molecular simulations. The computational tools allowed for the molecular-level investigation of the charge effect on the CO 2  diffusion in CAU-10-H.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Crystal Structure and Gas Adsorption of CAU-10-H
texts.text: 2.1. Crystal Structure and Gas Adsorption of CAU-10-H

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The crystal structure of a MOF determines its PLD, which is the critical property by which researchers have assessed molecular sieving capability. Despite the excellent stability of CAU-10-H in the  presence  of  moisture,  the  two  phases  of  CAU-10-H  corresponding to the dry and hydrated conditions with distinct PLD values have been reported. [18]   Based on the CIF obtained from the  Cambridge  Crystallographic  Data  Centre  (CCDC  deposition number: 1 454 067), the dry form of CAU-10-H possesses a PLD of roughly 3.7 Å, [18]   which is close to the kinetic diameter of  N 2 . [18,19] In  this  work,  we  computed  the  PLD  of  CAU-10-H using an open-source package, Zeo ++ , [20]  based on the CIF file corresponding to a given structure. Time-resolved X-ray diffraction  (XRD)  was  used  to  probe  the  conditions  associated  with structural  changes  from  as-synthesized  CAU-10-H  (hydrated form) to the dry form (Figure S1, Supporting Information). We observed that the phase change began after outgassing for 1 h and entirely transformed within 3 h, as indicated by a shift in (200) diffraction from 4.456 to 4.414 ° 2 θ . Note that in this work, the  as-synthesized  CAU-10-H  membrane  was  degassed  for more than 3 h prior to gas permeation testing, such that the dry form was likely the dominant phase in the membrane. In the subsequent discussion, we focus on the dry form of CAU-10-H and denote dry-form CAU-10-H as CAU-10-H.

texts.orig: www.afm-journal.de diffraction  method  with  the  semi-empirical  quantum  chemical method (PM7) and molecular  dynamic  (MD)  simulations.  The structure  of  CCDC  1  454  067  following  Rietveld  refinement (referred  to  as  Structure 1 in  this  study)  served  as  the  initial structure for the relaxation based on PM7 and MD calculations. Both  structural  relaxation  methods  revealed  remarkable  structural changes in the CAU-10-H, which led to a reduction in PLD to  less  than  3.7  Å  ( ≈ 3.2  Å  (PM7)  and ≈ 2.9  Å  (MD)). Figure 1 a illustrates the PLD evolution and Figure S2a,b, Supporting Information,  presents  representative  snapshots  obtained  from  the MD simulations. The structure of CAU-10-H following an iterative process involving PM7 optimization and Rietveld refinement (referred  to  as  Structure 2 )  is  illustrated  in  the  lower  panel  of Figure 1c. We expected that the notable difference between the PLD values of Structure 1 (3.7 Å) and Structure 2 (3.2 Å) would provide a distinct permeation cut-off for gas separation. The lattice  parameters of both structures are summarized in the Supporting  Information  (Table  S1,  Supporting  Information),  and their  pore  size  distributions  computed  using  Zeo ++ with  the corresponding CIF files are presented in Figure S2c, Supporting Information. While the cage size of both structures can be clearly indicated  in  the  pore  size  distributions  as  the  peak,  the  PLD cannot be identified in the same plot. It is because the narrowest spot of the channel, where PLD is determined, only occupies a very small portion of the pore volume of the entire structure.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Despite  notable  differences  in  the  PLD  values  of  Structures 1 and 2 ,  the  simulated  XRD  patterns  of  both  structures were in good agreement with their experimental counterparts (Figure  1b).  Furthermore,  the  Rietveld  refinement  of  the  two structures resulted in an excellent fit ( R wp   value < 10% as shown in Figure S3, Supporting Information). As discussed later, the  computed  permeation  properties  based  on  Structure 2 (PLD = 3.2  Å),  which  is  proposed  in  this  work,  more  closely matched the permeation properties observed in experiments.

texts.orig: Time-resolved  powder  XRD  was  also  performed  under  various gasses to investigate potential changes in the structure of CAU-10-H upon adsorption as well  as  its  adsorption  uptakes. The diffraction patterns measured in situ under various gasses (CO2, N2, and CH4) are summarized in Figure 2 a and Figure S4, Supporting  Information.  Note  that  the  time-resolved  patterns were  recorded  over  a  period  of  30  min  from  the  beginning of  a  stepped  increase  in  pressure  from  0.005  to  3  bar.  Actual time-resolved patterns under CO2 revealed that the diffraction intensity  of  the  (200)  peak  at  4.418 ° 2 θ dropped  considerably over  time.  This  intensity  reduction  is  an  indication  of  gas adsorption,  wherein  the  gas  molecules  occupying  the  pores decreased the overall contrast associated with the density of electrons from the MOF structure. [21]  The intensity changes in XRD patterns obtained under N2 and CH4 were not as pronounced as those obtained under CO2 (Figure S4, Supporting Information). These in situ XRD measurements suggest that the uptake of CO2 in CAU-10-H exceeded the uptake of N2 and CH4. These results were consistent with the adsorption isotherms obtained using the pressure-decay method (Figure S5, Supporting Information).  The  increased  CO 2   adsorption  uptake  can  be  attributed to its quadrupole moment, which has a strong interaction with aromatics in the linker of CAU-10-H. texts.orig: To confirm the absence of pinhole-type defects in the CAU10-H membrane, filtration was performed with dye molecules of  roughly  10  Å  (Rose  Bengal)  suspended  in  water.  Similar experiments  have  previously  been  used  to  examine  pinholes in membranes. [22]  Photographic images of the filtration results and  UV-vis  spectra  of  the  filtrate  solutions  are  presented in  the  Supporting  Information  (Figure  S12,  Supporting  Information).  Energy  dispersive  spectroscopy  (EDS)  was  used  to detect  the  signal  from  aluminum  and  iodine  on  the  surface and  in  the  cross-section  of  the  membrane  (Figure  3b).  The aluminum  signal  was  contributed  by  CAU-10-H  and  the  alumina substrate beneath the CAU-10-H membrane, whereas the iodine  signal  was  attributed  to  the  dye  molecules.  The  CAU10-H seed layer sample presented iodine signals on the surface and throughout the entire cross-section. The dense CAU-10-H membrane prepared via secondary growth presented almost no iodine signals beneath the top surface of the membrane, indicating an absence of detectable pinholes exceeding 10 Å.

texts.orig: Figure 4b compares the CO2 separation performance of the CAU-10-H  membrane  and  several  previously  reported  pure MOF  membranes  in  terms  of  Robeson  plots  quantified  as selectivity  and  permeability. [14,24]   The  dense  CAU-10-H  membrane clearly outperformed existing pure MOF membranes in terms  of  CO 2 /CH4  selectivity.  This  membrane  also  possessed higher  CO2/N2  selectivity  than  most  pure  MOF  membranes, except for the MOF-5 membrane. [24l]  However, several previous reports  pointed  out  that  MOF-5  has  low  ideal  selectivity  for CO2/N2, and the high selectivity only appears in a short range of  the  feed  composition. [25]   One  recent  work  on  membranes derived from ZIF-62 revealed high CO2 separation performance (36.6  for  CO 2 /CH4  and  34.5  for  CO 2 /N2). [13c]   That  membrane underwent a  thermal  treatment  at  roughly  400 ° C  to  create  a glassy  phase  from  ZIF-62,  meaning  that  the  excellent  separation performance could be attributed to the amorphous phase rather  than  the  MOF  crystals.  The  promising  CO 2   separation performance of the dense CAU-10-H membranes in this study can be attributed to the intrinsic crystalline phase of CAU-10-H. Note that the CAU-10-H membrane achieved the highest CO2 permeability  (1.7 × 10 -13   mol  m  m -2 s -1 Pa -1 = 500  Barrer  for single-gas permeation and 7 .5 × 10 -13  mol m m -2 s -1  Pa -1 = 220 Barrer  for  mixed-gas  permeation)  among  existing  pure  MOF membranes with a CO2/CH4 selectivity exceeding 30. We also compared the separation performance of the CAU-10-H membrane to the emerging GO membranes (Figure S13, Supporting Information).  The  GO  membranes  usually  show  low  permeability, because the permeation of gases relies on the diffusion through a tortuous pathway between the layered structure.

texts.orig: 2.3. Gas Transport through CAU-10-H at the Molecular Level
texts.text: 2.3. Gas Transport through CAU-10-H at the Molecular Level

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Molecular simulations were conducted to gain insight into the excellent CO2 separation performance of the CAU-10-H. Monte Carlo simulations were used to map the Helmholtz free energy profile of molecules inside the permeation channel-note that CAU-10-H has a 1D channel along the c -axis.  The  free  energy profile  was  subsequently  used  to  estimate  the  CH 4 self-diffusivity under infinitely dilute conditions based on transition state theory  (Equation  (6)  in  Section  4)  to  approximate  the  Fickian (transport) diffusivity. [26]   Molecular dynamics simulations were employed to acquire the diffusivity of CO 2  and N 2 . As expected, the computed self-diffusivity of N 2  and CH 4  through Structure 2 (PLD = 3.2 Å) was lower than that through Structure 1 (PLD = 3.7 Å) (Figure S14, Supporting Information). Surprisingly, the calculated  self-diffusivity  of  CO 2 was  one  order  of  magnitude higher in Structure 2 than in Structure 1 despite a smaller PLD (3.2 vs 3.7 Å). This counter-intuitive finding can be attributed to the charge distribution in CAU-10-H. This Coulombic effect is discussed later in the manuscript.

Morphology of pinholes in dense CAU-10-H membrane. a) SEM image of CAU-10-H seed layer (top), top-view image of dense CAU-10-H membrane following secondary growth (middle), and side-view of the same (bottom). b) Filtration of dye molecules (Rose Bengal) was used to probe the presence of pinholes in the seed layer and dense CAU-10-H membrane. Cross-sectional SEM images (first column) and elemental mapping via EDS (second and third column) of the CAU-10-H seed layer (top row) as well as the dense membrane (bottom row), following dye filtration are presented. texts.orig: The  product of computed  diffusivity and experimentally obtained  solubility  from  adsorption  isotherms  (Figure  S5,  Supporting Information) represented the computed permeability of the  CAU-10-H  membrane.  The  ratio  of  the  computed  permeability  between  two  gases  yielded  the  computed  selectivity.  The computed  selectivity  based  on  Structure 2 was  in  reasonable agreement with the experiment results ( Figure 5 a). In the case of Structure 1 (PLD = 3.7 Å), the computed selectivity did not match the experimental observations, which predicted reverse CO 2  selectivity.  A  comparison of experiment and simulation results indicated that the structure of CAU-10-H was more closely represented by Structure 2 (PLD = 3.2 Å) than by Structure 1 (PLD = 3.7 Å). We note that Structure 2 is proposed by this study.

texts.orig: Figure 5b summarizes the free energy profiles of the three gases through the channel of CAU-10-H. The free energy barrier  infers  the  hopping  rate  of  penetrants  between  adjacent cages (Equation (6))-a high free energy barrier indicates low diffusivity and vice versa. In the following, we discuss the free energy  profiles  in  Figure  5b  (middle  row),  which  were  computed  from  simulations  considering  van  der  Waals  and  Coulombic interactions. In Structure 1 ,  the  free  energy barrier for CO2 was higher than that for N2 and CH4, which suggests that this  structure  does  not  adequately  describe  the  separation  of CO2 over N2 or CH4. By contrast, in Structure 2 , the free energy barrier for CO 2  was lower than for N 2  or CH4, which makes it a more reasonable structure.

texts.orig: However,  we  note  an  unexpected  lower  free  energy  barrier for  CO 2 diffusion  found  in  Structure 2 as  opposed  to  Structure 1 , despite a smaller PLD for Structure 2 (3.2 vs 3.7 Å). We hypothesize  that  this  can  be  attributed  to  the  favorable  Coulombic  effect.  We  tested  this  hypothesis  by  computing  free energy  profiles  without  considering  Coulombic  interactions; that  is,  we  only  considered  the  contribution  of  van  der  Waals forces (bottom row of Figure 5b). As expected, in the absence of  the  Coulombic  interaction,  the  free  energy  barrier  for  CO 2 in  Structure 1 (PLD = 3.7  Å)  was  lower  than  in  Structure 2 (PLD = 3.2  Å).  Taking  the  Coulombic interaction into account raised the free energy barrier for CO 2  in both structures; nevertheless, the effect was less pronounced in Structure 2 .

texts.orig: To  gain  further  insight  into  this  counter-intuitive  finding, we sought to differentiate the contributions of internal energy and  entropy  to  the  free  energy  barrier  of  CO 2   in  CAU-10-H ( Figure 6 a). The methodology used to calculate the differences in  internal  energy  and  entropy  in  the  cage  and  those  at  the transport bottleneck of CAU-10-H is elaborated in Figure S15, Supporting  Information.  The  results  in  Figure  6a  revealed almost no difference in entropic barrier ( -T ∆ S ) in Structures 1 and 2 , regardless of the Coulombic effect. Thus, it appears that the difference in internal energy in the cage and at the bottleneck ( ∆ U ) dominated the free energy barrier ( ∆ A ). These findings  are  in  agreement  with  previous  MOF  systems,  in  which internal energy dominated gas permeation. [27]

texts.orig: Figure 6b presents the computed average internal energy of CO2 in the cage and at the bottleneck to elucidate how ∆ U varied in Structures 1 and 2 . The average internal energy of CO 2  in the cages of the two structures was almost identical, with or without the  Coulombic  effect;  however,  the  average  internal  energy  of CO2 at  the  bottleneck  was  sensitive  to  the  charge  assignment in  CAU-10-H.  In  the  absence  of  the  Coulombic  contribution, internal  energy  of  CO 2 at  the  bottleneck  was  similar  in  Structures 1 and 2 ( -8.6  k B T),  despite  the  0.5  Å  difference  in  PLD. The bottleneck in the CAU-10-H was defined by the hydrogen atoms in the organic linker, and the small van der Waals radius of hydrogen would not necessarily account for the high energy barrier  in  Structure 2 (PLD = 3.2  Å).  With  the  assignment  of charges,  the  internal  energy  of  CO 2   increased  considerably from -8.6 to -4.5 k B T at the bottleneck in Structure 1 , whereas it  increased  only  to -6.9  k B T  at  the  bottleneck  in  Structure 2 . The Coulombic effect introduced a repulsive interaction to the transport  of  CO 2 through  both  structures;  however,  the  effect was less pronounced in Structure 2 ,  which had a smaller PLD than Structure 1 .  The lower electrostatic potential surface near the bottleneck in Structure 2 resulted in a lower energy pathway for CO2, as indicated in the internal energy profile in Figure 6b and in Figure S16, Supporting Information. In other words, the Coulombic effect of the structure with a smaller opening at the bottleneck  facilitated  the  diffusion  of  CO 2   through  CAU-10-H, resulting in good CO2 /CH4  separation performance.

texts.orig: 3. Conclusions and Outlook
texts.text: 3. Conclusions and Outlook

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This paper reports on the fabrication of a defect-free CAU-10-H membrane with high ideal selectivity for CO 2 /N 2  (42) and CO 2 / CH4 (95). Note that few MOF membranes provide the selectivity required  for  industrial  applications  (selectivity  of > 30).  More importantly,  the  CO 2   permeability  of  the  proposed  CAU-10-H membrane  (1.7 × 10 -13   mol  m  m -2 s -1 Pa -1 ≈ 500  Barrer)  is higher than that of all existing MOF membranes with CO2/CH4 selectivity  over  30.  This  outstanding  CO2/CH4 separation performance could greatly facilitate the purification of natural gas, which is among the cleanest and most efficient fuels. Our investigation into the molecular transport in CAU-10-H revealed that the  previously  reported  PLD  of  3.7  Å  (Structure 1 )  is  not  necessarily  an  accurate description of the structure. We proposed a  more  realistic  structure  with  a  PLD  of  3.2  Å  (Structure 2 ). Molecular  simulations  indicated  that  the  free  energy  barrier for the diffusion of CO 2  was higher in Structure 1 ,  despite  its larger PLD. This counter-intuitive observation can be attributed to  Coulombic  effects,  which  created  a  relatively  energetically favorable pathway in Structure 2 . Our finding suggests that the Coulombic interactions are a critical factor determining the diffusion of CO2; and the opening of the bottleneck in MOFs may not serve as an adequate indicator for evaluating the CO 2  separation performance. The molecular-level phenomena described in  this  study  could  be  used  in  the  engineering  of  other

texts.orig: Figure 6. Detailed investigation into the Coulombic effect on the transport of CO 2  through CAU-10-H. a) Change in Helmholtz free energy, internal energy, and entropy of CO2 hopping from cage to adjacent bottleneck. These quantities were computed from Monte Carlo simulations, the details of which are elaborated on in Figure S15, Supporting Information. We found that Coulombic interaction considerably increased the energetic barrier to diffusion of CO 2 , and the effect was more pronounced in Structure 1 (with a larger PLD). Structure 2 presented a lower energy barrier to diffusion of CO2. b) Upper panel shows the average internal energy of CO2 in the cage (dark green) and at the bottleneck (orange). The gray arrow and text highlight the increase in internal energy at the bottleneck after accounting for the Coulombic interaction. At the bottleneck, Structure 2 (with a smaller PLD) presented the internal energy lower than those of Structure 1 , due to the Coulombic effect. The lower panel presents the internal energy profiles of CO2 in the two CAU-10-H structures. Note that the bottleneck region is located between the parallel dashed lines. A relatively energetically favorable pathway created by the Coulombic effect appeared in Structure 2 .  Note that the position of the bottleneck in each structure was based on the free energy profile; that is, it is not necessarily the narrowest spot along the channel. Details pertaining to the definition of the bottleneck in the CAU-10-H structures can be found in Figure S15, Supporting Information.

texts.orig: Synthesis  of  CAU-10-H  in  Powder  Form :  Al 2 (SO4)3·18H2O  (3.332  g, 5 mmol) and 1,3-H2BTC (0.8306 g, 5 mmol) were dissolved in a mixed solvent of DI water (24 mL) and DMF (6 mL). The solution was stirred and  refluxed  at  120 ° C  for  2  days.  White  powder  formed  during  the reaction  (i.e.,  solid-state  reaction  product)  was  collected  via  vacuum filtration using DI water for washing. Activation of as-synthesized CAU10-H  (i.e.,  removal  of  DMF  from  the  micropores)  was  achieved  by immersing powder samples in methanol (50 mL) at room temperature under  agitation  for  1  day.  The  samples  were  collected  via  vacuum filtration and dried at 105 ° C for 1 day prior to use.

The  cast  solution  was  prepared by dispersing CAU-10-H power (0.02  g)  in  DI  water  (10  mL).  3  mL  of  the  CAU-10-H  suspension  was dropped  on  the α -alumina  substrate  for  the  spin-on  deposition  at 1000  rpm  for  a  period  of  30  s  using  a  Laurell  spin-coater  (Model-WS650MZ-23NPPB). The above-mentioned deposition cycle  was  repeated two times in order to improve the coverage of CAU-10-H crystals on the substrate. Specimens were then baked in an oven at 105 ° C for 20 min.

texts.orig: Secondary Growth of CAU-10-H Membrane : Secondary growth involved placing α -alumina substrates seeded with CAU-10-H crystals in a 500-mL flask filled with 90 mL of the solution. The setup is illustrated in  the  Supporting  Information  (Figure  S17,  Supporting  Information). The  solution  used  for  secondary  growth  consisted  of  Al2(SO4)3·18H2O (1.666 g, 2.5 mmol), 1,3-H 2 BTC (0.4153 g, 2.5 mmol), DI water (72 mL), and  DMF  (18  mL).  The  flask  was  heated  to  100 ° C  and  held  at  this temperature for 6 h without agitation to enable the growth of a dense CAU-10-H  membrane  on  the  substrate.  Following  the  reaction,  the entire substrate was rinsed with DI water to remove particles that were physically  attached  to  the  surface  of  the  substrate.  The  as-made  CAU10-H samples were activated by immersing them in 100 mL of methanol for 12 h at room temperature without agitation. The samples were then dried in an oven at 70 ° C for at least 1 day prior to use.

texts.orig: X-Ray  Diffraction  In-House  Setup :  Grazing-incidence  X-ray  diffraction (GIXRD) for the membrane samples were conducted using a PAnalytical X'pert diffractometer with Cu K α radiation at a wavelength of 1.5418 Å. Each GIXRD pattern was scanned from 5 ° to  30 ° 2 θ with  a  step  size  of  0.02 ° 2 θ using an incident beam of 1 ° . The 2D XRD pattern was obtained using Rigaku Smartlab SE diffractometer with Cu K α radiation at a wavelength of 1.5418 Å. The diffraction pattern was acquired with HyPix-400 detector. The obtained diffraction pattern was processed using Smart Studio II software.

texts.orig: XRD  Analysis  Using  Synchrotron  Radiation  Source :  Time-resolved  in situ X-ray diffraction (XRD) for measurements of CAU-10-H dehydration and it being exposed to CO 2, N2, or CH4 was conducted at the Taiwan Photon Source (TPS) 09A station at the National Synchrotron Radiation Research  Center  (NSRRC).  The  wavelength  of  the  incident  X-rays  was 0.826569  Å  (15  keV),  delivered  from  an  in-vacuum  undulator  (IU22). The diffraction patterns were recorded using a MYTHEN 24K positionsensitive  detector.  The  powder  sample  was  packed  in  a  capillary  tube with  a  diameter  of  0.7  mm.  Measurements  were  performed  using  a proprietary setup (Figure S18, Supporting Information).  CAU-10-H dehydration  was  determined  from  XRD  patterns  after  outgassing  the entire  system  under  0.005  bar  at  30 ° C.  XRD  patterns  were  recorded at  intervals  of  20  s  using  an  exposure  time  of  5  s.  The  adsorption  of various gases on CAU-10-H and corresponding structural changes were characterized  after  degassing  the  entire  system  under  0.005  bar  for 6 h. XRD patterns were acquired after respectively introducing the gasses (CO2,  N2,  or  CH 4 )  into  the  capillary  tube  under  3  bar  at  30 ° C.  XRD patterns were recorded at intervals of 20 s using an exposure time of 5 s.

texts.orig: Pinhole Characterization : Membrane  samples  were  subjected to filtration with dye molecules (Rose Bengal) for the detection of pinholes. The  setup  is  illustrated  in  Figure  S19,  Supporting  Information.  The upstream of the membrane was in contact with an aqueous solution of Rose Bengal (0.05 mol L -1 ), while DI water was filled at the downstream of  the  membrane.  Dye  molecules  of  roughly  1  nm  were  hypothesized to  be  able  to  pass  through  pinholes  of > 1  nm.  The  distribution  of  dye on the top surface and in cross-sections of the membrane sample was characterized using energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) attached to a Hitachi  S-4800  SEM.  A  ultraviolet-visible  (UV-vis)  spectrophotometer (Agilent Cary 100) was used to quantify the concentration of Rose Bengal on the permeate side.

texts.orig: Computational  Approaches :  Molecular  dynamics  (MD)  simulations were  used  as  one  of  the  measures  for  structural  optimization.  This series of simulations were implemented in the canonical ensemble (i.e., NVT)  at  300  K  using  the  open-source  LAMMPS  software  package. [29] A  starting  superstructure  containing  16  channels  was  constructed  by replicating the structure of CCDC# 1 454 067 (PLD = 3.70  Å).  A  NoséHoover thermostat was used with a damping factor of 100 time-steps (i.e., 100 fs). The atomic charges of atoms in the framework were previously derived using the  Electronegativity  Equalization  Method  (EEM). The L-J parameters between different  types  of  atom  were  estimated  using  the  Lorentz-Berthelot mixing  rules.  The  configurations  of  MD  simulations  were  collected  to 20 ps with a time interval of 0.2 ps.

texts.orig: PM7, [32] implemented in MOPAC 2016, [33] was a different computational  tool  used  in  this  study  for  the  structural  optimization. The PM7 method has been parametrized to minimize error with respect to  experimental  and  high-level  ab  initio  reference  data  in  terms  of crystal structures and heats of formation. In these PM7 calculations, all atomic positions were relaxed until the gradient norm dropped to below 0.01 kcal mol -1  Å -1  (i.e., GNORM = 0.01).

texts.orig: The  Widom  particle  insertion  method [34] was  used  to  probe  the Helmholtz  free  energy  distribution  of  CO 2 ,  N 2 ,  and  CH 4   in  CAU-10-H along  the  permeation  direction  (i.e., c -axis) under  infinite dilution conditions. In these calculations, the 6-12 Lennard-Jones (L-J) potential was  used,  truncated  and  shifted  at  a  cutoff  radius  of  12.8  Å,  and long-range  Coulombic  interactions  were  computed  using  the  Ewald summation  technique  to  describe intermolecular interactions. The simulation box was composed of multiple unit cells to ensure at least 2 times of the cutoff radius in each direction. For atoms in MOFs, the L-J parameters were based on the universal force field (UFF) using the same atomic charges mentioned in the MD simulations above. The LorentzBerthelot  mixing  rule  was  used  for  the  assignment  of  L-J  parameters between dissimilar atoms. The temperature of the simulations was set at 303 K.

texts.orig: The  definitions  of  the  cage  and  bottleneck  in  the  two  structures are  presented  in  the  Supporting  Information  (Figure  S15,  Supporting Information).  The  average  internal  energy  of  CO 2 in  the  cage  or bottleneck  was  computed  using  the  Bolzmann-weighted  local  internal energy of possible CO2 configurations within the respective regions. The difference in the average internal energy of CO 2  in the cage and at the bottleneck  was  defined  as  the  barrier  of  internal  energy  ( ∆ U )  for  CO 2 transport from the cage to the bottleneck. The entropic barrier ( -T ∆ S ) for CO2 was then computed as ∆ U -∆ A .
